question:
A 6-year-old boy — left upper eyelid lower since birth (fixed, no periodicity). MRD I = 3 mm, levator elevation = 6 mm , good Bell's phenomenon, normal motility.

What is your full diagnosis?
answer: Congenital simple ptosis of the left upper eyelid — Moderate degree

question:
A 6-year-old boy — left upper eyelid lower since birth (fixed, no periodicity). MRD I = 3 mm, levator elevation = 6 mm , good Bell's phenomenon, normal motility.

Name the suitable surgical procedure.
answer: Levator resection and advancement — Blascovic's (conjunctival) or Everbusch's (skin) operation

question:
A 6-year-old boy — left upper eyelid lower since birth (fixed, no periodicity). MRD I = 3 mm, levator elevation = 6 mm , good Bell's phenomenon, normal motility.

Will you intervene surgically at age 6? Would your decision differ if the child was 2 years old? Justify.
answer: At 6 years: ✅ Yes — Mild to moderate ptosis → wait until school age (5–6 years). Pupil not fully occluded (MRD I = 3 mm), so amblyopia risk is lower. Timing is now appropriate. At 2 years: Decision differs only if ptosis were severe . In this moderate case (MRD I = 3 mm, pupil partially visible) → still safe to wait. Rule: Severe ptosis (pupil fully covered, MRD I = 0) → operate as early as possible to prevent stimulus deprivation amblyopia. Moderate → wait to school age.

question:
A 6-year-old boy — left upper eyelid lower since birth (fixed, no periodicity). MRD I = 3 mm, levator elevation = 6 mm , good Bell's phenomenon, normal motility.

If MRD I = 0 mm and levator function = 2 mm — best surgical option?

Classify ectropion by severity with description of each grade. Enumerate lines of treatment of the paralytic type .

Classify ectropion by severity (3 grades)
answer: Mild: Lacrimal punctum is visible and everted → causes epiphora. Tarsal conjunctiva NOT yet exposed. Moderate: Tarsal conjunctiva exposed → becomes hyperemic, dry, keratinized. Severe: Conjunctival fornix completely exposed → lagophthalmos → exposure keratopathy.

question:
Classify ectropion by severity with description of each grade. Enumerate lines of treatment of the paralytic type .

Lines of treatment — paralytic ectropion
answer: A) Conservative (while awaiting nerve recovery): Vitamin B complex, vasodilators, steroids → promote nerve regeneration Physiotherapy (massage + electrical stimulation) Lubricating drops/ointment + lid taping at night B) Surgical (permanent palsy): Lateral tarsorrhaphy Canthoplasty (canthal tendon tightening) Lateral tarsal strip procedure Fascial sling

question: Define: Hordeolum externum
answer: Hordeolum externum (Stye): Acute suppurative (bacterial) inflammation of the Zeiss gland (sebaceous gland at eyelash follicle) at the eyelid margin.

question:
Regarding chronic dacryocystitis : causative organism / symptom & sign / two surgical procedures.

Main causative organism
answer: Pneumococcus (Streptococcus pneumoniae) — responsible for 80% of cases.

question:
Regarding chronic dacryocystitis : causative organism / symptom & sign / two surgical procedures.

One symptom + one sign
answer: Symptom: Epiphora (watering) with mucopurulent discharge Sign: Positive regurgitation test — pressing the lacrimal sac area causes mucus/pus to regurgitate from the punctum, confirming NLD obstruction.

question:
Regarding chronic dacryocystitis : causative organism / symptom & sign / two surgical procedures.

Two surgical procedures in adults
answer: Dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) — definitive treatment. Connects lacrimal sac directly to nasal mucosa of middle meatus, bypassing the obstructed NLD. Dacryocystectomy — complete excision of the lacrimal sac. Indicated in: TB of sac, neoplasm, atrophy/fibrosis, or with associated hypopyon corneal ulcer.

question: 6-month baby — left watery eye despite antibiotics & massage. Large, cloudy left cornea with bluish scleral hue. Pediatrician diagnosed CNLDO — is he correct?
answer: No — The pediatrician is WRONG. Correct diagnosis: Congenital (primary infantile) glaucoma — Buphthalmos

question: Give the principle of Dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) operation.
answer: Create a new direct communication (fistula) between the lacrimal sac and the nasal mucosa of the middle meatus , bypassing the obstructed nasolacrimal duct.

question: Enumerate 6 complications of trachoma .
answer: Trachoma (Chlamydia trachomatis) → chronic conjunctival scarring (cicatrization): Lid complications: Entropion — inturning of lid margin (most common lid complication) Trichiasis — inturning lashes → corneal abrasion Ptosis — levator damage + lid weight from scarring Lagophthalmos — inability to close eye due to scarring Corneal complications: Pannus — fibrovascular membrane from superior limbus → corneal opacity Corneal ulceration / opacity — from trichiasis + entropion → visual loss/blindness

question:
Regarding purulent conjunctivitis : Why are corneal ulcers common? / Modes of transmission.

Why are corneal ulcers common?
answer: Gonococci can invade an intact corneal epithelium (unique property — unlike other bacteria needing a breach) Chemosis → presses on limbal capillaries → impairs corneal nutrition → pools organisms near cornea Proteolytic enzymes from leucocytes + gonococcal exotoxins damage stroma

question:
Regarding purulent conjunctivitis : Why are corneal ulcers common? / Modes of transmission.

Modes of transmission
answer: Eye-to-eye — direct contact via hands/towels/fomites Auto-inoculation from genital gonorrhea via fingers Ophthalmia neonatorum — neonate passing through infected birth canal

question: Differentiate (table) between papillae of vernal conjunctivitis vs trachoma — Etiology / Size / Upper fornix / Discharge / Inclusion bodies.
answer: Feature VKC Papillae Trachoma Papillae Etiology Allergic (IgE-mediated) — non-infectious Infective — Chlamydia trachomatis (serotypes A,B,Ba,C) Size Large, flat-topped, dark red/bluish — cobblestone, central vascular tuft Small–moderate, not flat-topped, regular reddish elevations Upper fornix Free / spared — papillae only on upper tarsus Involved — primarily affects upper tarsal conjunctiva AND fornix Discharge Thick, ropy (stringy) mucoid — rich in eosinophils Mucopurulent (watery to frankly purulent) Inclusion bodies Absent — allergic condition, no organisms Present — Halberstaedter–Prowazek (HP) bodies on Giemsa stain

question: Define: Cotton-wool spots
answer: Cotton-wool spots = microinfarctions (ischemic infarcts) of the nerve fiber layer of the retina, caused by retinal arteriole occlusion.

question:
30-year-old male — 10–15 days blurred vision after trauma with vegetable matter . Corneal ulcer: soft creamy infiltrate, feathery margins , satellite lesions.

Name the causative organism
answer: Filamentous fungus — most commonly Fusarium species

question:
30-year-old male — 10–15 days blurred vision after trauma with vegetable matter . Corneal ulcer: soft creamy infiltrate, feathery margins , satellite lesions.

Detection methods
answer: Specimen: Corneal scraping (base + edges of ulcer) under slit-lamp guidance 1) Microscopy: 10% KOH wet mount — fastest; reveals fungal hyphae directly (dissolves debris) Gram stain — hyphae as gram-positive branching filaments Giemsa stain — shows fungal elements + inflammatory cells Calcofluor white (fluorescent) — binds chitin; highly sensitive PAS stain — highlights fungal walls magenta 2) Culture: Sabouraud's dextrose agar (SDA) — standard fungal medium; 25–30°C; 4–6 weeks 3) Confocal microscopy — non-invasive in vivo detection of hyphae in stroma

question:
30-year-old male — 10–15 days blurred vision after trauma with vegetable matter . Corneal ulcer: soft creamy infiltrate, feathery margins , satellite lesions.

Lines of treatment
answer: Medical (first-line): Natamycin 5% — drug of choice for filamentous fungi (q1–2h initially) Voriconazole 1% — better stromal penetration; broad spectrum Systemic voriconazole/itraconazole — deep/severe cases Cycloplegics (atropine) — relieve spasm, prevent synechiae ⚠️ Absolutely contraindicated: Topical steroids — enhance fungal growth dramatically.

question:
Regarding dendritic corneal ulcers : causative organism / 3 complications (non-perforated) / most potent antiviral.

Causative organism

Regarding dendritic corneal ulcers : causative organism / 3 complications (non-perforated) / most potent antiviral.

3 complications of non-perforated dendritic ulcer
answer: Geographic ulcer — dendritic ulcer enlarges → large amoeboid epithelial defect (especially if steroids used mistakenly) Disciform / stromal keratitis — immune-mediated reaction to viral antigens in stroma → stromal edema, infiltration, vascularization → permanent scarring Neurotrophic keratitis (Meta-herpetic ulcer) — repeated episodes damage corneal nerves (V1) → anesthetic cornea → persistent non-healing trophic ulcer Also accepted: Corneal scarring/opacity; secondary bacterial infection; herpetic iridocyclitis

question:
Regarding dendritic corneal ulcers : causative organism / 3 complications (non-perforated) / most potent antiviral.

Most potent antiviral agent
answer: Acyclovir (Zovirax) — "the most recent and most potent antiviral drug" (textbook)

question:
Define: Neurotrophic keratitis / River green sign (Seidel test)

Neurotrophic keratitis
answer: Non-infective keratitis caused by absence of corneal sensation (paralysis of ophthalmic division of V1) → central disc-shaped, painless ulcer → tends to persist and perforate without treatment.

question:
Define: Neurotrophic keratitis / River green sign (Seidel test)

River green sign (Seidel test)
answer: Sign of corneal perforation — fluorescein instilled → aqueous leaking through perforation dilutes the dye → streaming light-green "river" under cobalt blue light against dark-orange background of concentrated dye = confirms active aqueous leakage.

question: Keratoconus: pinhole improved right eye (6/60 → 6/9) but did NOT improve left eye. Explain why.
answer: Right eye (improved): Regular refractive error (myopia/regular astigmatism). Pinhole blocks peripheral oblique rays → allows only narrow central beam → eliminates blur circle → sharper retinal image. Confirms optical/refractive cause. Left eye (did NOT improve): Two simultaneous problems: Irregular astigmatism — keratoconus cone → light from different meridians focuses at multiple points. Pinhole cannot fix this because the irregularity is at the corneal surface, not axial length/regular curvature. Apical corneal opacity — scar tissue blocks/scatters/distorts light regardless of what's placed in front of the eye. Only hard RGP contact lenses can correct irregular astigmatism (they neutralize the corneal surface). Pinhole = same limitation as spectacles.

question: Describe signs of hypermature senile cortical cataract on slit-lamp examination (fully dilated pupil).
answer: Liquefied milky cortex — autolysis → milky white fluid fills capsular bag Sinking nucleus (Morgagnian cataract) — denser nucleus sinks inferiorly in liquefied cortex → brown amber opacity at inferior pole (pathognomonic) Shrunken / wrinkled lens capsule — volume reduction → wrinkled, calcified capsule Reappearance of iris shadow — shrinking lens → clear zone between iris and opacity → pseudo-immature appearance Iridodenesis (tremulous iris) — lost lens support → iris shimmers with eye movement Deep anterior chamber — shrinking lens moves posteriorly Phacolytic changes — leaked lens proteins → macrophages laden with lens material visible in AC (phacolytic glaucoma)

question: Senile nuclear sclerosis: describe clinical picture — Visual acuity / Oblique illumination / Red reflex .
answer: Visual acuity: Correctable to normal with glasses. Causes progressive index myopia → "second sight" (patient can temporarily read without glasses). Deteriorates with progression. Oblique illumination: Nucleus appears grayish (vs nuclear cataract = yellowish-brown to black). Iris shadow initially positive. Red reflex: Entirely red/uniform throughout — no central darkening (vs nuclear cataract = dark center on red periphery). Nuclear sclerosis insufficient to block reflex.

question: Give the principle of Extracapsular Cataract Extraction (ECCE) .
answer: Surgical delivery of the lens nucleus through an anterior capsulotomy + irrigation/aspiration of soft cortex, while leaving the intact posterior capsule in place .

question: 30-year-old female: right eye 6/6 (+1D), left eye 6/60 not improved with refraction (+7D, never wore glasses). Name and explain the non-improvement.

15-year-old girl — acute onset right painful proptosis , ophthalmoplegia, swollen hyperemic lids and conjunctiva. Left eye normal. Temp 39°C.

Probable diagnosis
answer: Right Orbital Cellulitis — acute suppurative inflammation of orbital fibrofatty tissue posterior to the orbital septum.

question:
15-year-old girl — acute onset right painful proptosis , ophthalmoplegia, swollen hyperemic lids and conjunctiva. Left eye normal. Temp 39°C.

4 potential complications
answer: Ocular/Orbital: Orbital abscess — pus collection requiring surgical drainage Exposure keratopathy — proptosis → incomplete lid closure → corneal ulceration CRAO/CRVO — pressure on orbital vessels → acute vision loss Panophthalmitis — infection extends into globe Intracranial: Cavernous sinus thrombosis / Meningitis / Brain abscess / Septicemia

question:
15-year-old girl — acute onset right painful proptosis , ophthalmoplegia, swollen hyperemic lids and conjunctiva. Left eye normal. Temp 39°C.

2 differential diagnoses
answer: Cavernous sinus thrombosis — similar but characteristically bilateral proptosis + meningeal signs. Orbital cellulitis = unilateral. Preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis — lid swelling + redness BUT no proptosis, no ophthalmoplegia, no vision loss . Infection anterior to orbital septum.

question: Formulate a clinical approach to unilateral proptosis .
answer: Definition: Forward displacement >20 mm from lateral orbital rim to corneal apex, or >2 mm asymmetry between the two eyes. History: Onset (acute = hemorrhage/infection; slow = benign tumor), course (progressive/regressive/intermittent), pain, diplopia, visual loss, thyroid symptoms. Examination: Full ophthalmologic exam (VA, IOP, pupil RAPD, fundus) Confirm + measure: Hertel exophthalmometer Direction: axial = intraconal; displaced = extraconal Lid lag → thyroid; Pulsating → CCF; Thrill/bruit → CCF Investigations: B-scan US, CT orbit (bone/sinus), MRI (soft tissue/optic nerve), thyroid profile, CBC. Differential Diagnosis: Category Examples Endocrine Thyroid ophthalmopathy (most common) Developmental Dermoid cyst Traumatic Retrobulbar hemorrhage Inflammatory Orbital cellulitis (acute), pseudotumor (chronic) Vascular Orbital varices, carotid-cavernous fistula Neoplastic Hemangioma (benign), rhabdomyosarcoma, metastases

question: Demonstrate the difference in refractive state between myopic and hypermetropic eye (with illustrations).
answer: Myopic Eye: Eye too long or cornea/lens too curved → parallel rays focus BEFORE the retina → blurred distance vision, clear near. Corrected with concave (minus) lens. Parallel rays → Focus BEFORE retina → Blur |||→→→ [Cornea/Lens] → •focus → [Retina] ✗

question: Describe methods of treatment of hypermetropia in an adult patient .
answer: 1) Optical: Spectacle glasses (convex/plus lenses) — converges parallel rays onto retina Contact lenses (soft or RGP) 2) Refractive Surgery: LASIK — flap + excimer laser steepens central cornea (most common) PRK — epithelium removed + direct laser ablation Femto-SMILE — flapless femtosecond technique Phakic IOL — additional lens implanted while keeping natural lens (high hypermetropia) Refractive Lens Exchange (RLE) — natural lens removed + high-power IOL; best for older/presbyopic patients

question: Presbyope: sees 6/6 with -1D for far. Near point with far glasses = 33 cm. Needs to focus at 20 cm for work. Calculate near glasses.
answer: Step Calculation Result Amplitude of accommodation 100 ÷ 33 cm ≈ 3 D Reserve 1/3 1/3 × 3 D 1 D (reserved) Available accommodation 3 D − 1 D 2 D Power needed for 20 cm 100 ÷ 20 5 D Addition needed 5 D − 2 D +3 D Near glasses −1 D + (+3 D) +2.00 D

question:
14-year-old boy — difficulty reading blackboard, no difficulty reading books, recurrent headache. Right VA 6/24 → 6/9 with PH; Left VA 6/18 → 6/9 with PH. Diagnosis + explain pinhole improvement.

Diagnosis

14-year-old boy — difficulty reading blackboard, no difficulty reading books, recurrent headache. Right VA 6/24 → 6/9 with PH; Left VA 6/18 → 6/9 with PH. Diagnosis + explain pinhole improvement.

Explain pinhole improvement mechanism
answer: Pinhole blocks peripheral (paraaxial) divergent rays — in myopia, peripheral rays are most aberrant and cause greatest blur on retina The narrow central beam creates a tiny circle of diffusion on the retina → effectively sharper image Improvement with PH = optical/refractive cause (not amblyopia, retinal/optic nerve disease — those would NOT improve).

question:
6-month baby — left watery eye, large cloudy cornea, bluish scleral hue. Congenital glaucoma (Buphthalmos) workup.

Next step?
answer: Measure IOP under general anesthesia (EUA — Examination Under Anesthesia)

question:
6-month baby — left watery eye, large cloudy cornea, bluish scleral hue. Congenital glaucoma (Buphthalmos) workup.

3 causes of cloudy cornea in an infant
answer: Congenital glaucoma — corneal edema from elevated IOP + Haab's striae CHED (Congenital Hereditary Endothelial Dystrophy) — primary endothelial dysfunction → bilateral ground-glass opacity from birth; normal IOP Birth trauma — forceps injury → Descemet's rupture + corneal edema Also accepted: Sclerocornea; interstitial keratitis

question:
6-month baby — left watery eye, large cloudy cornea, bluish scleral hue. Congenital glaucoma (Buphthalmos) workup.

Management of congenital glaucoma
answer: Surgery is the primary treatment — medical treatment is only temporizing.

question:
Regarding Buphthalmos : 2 corneal signs / 2 complications / 2 surgical procedures.

2 corneal signs
answer: Corneal enlargement (megalocornea) — elevated IOP stretches infant eye; diameter >12 mm (suspicious >11 mm) Haab's striae — horizontal/concentric Descemet's membrane ruptures from stretching → heal as grayish lines + corneal opacity Third sign: Corneal edema from endothelial decompensation

question:
Regarding Buphthalmos : 2 corneal signs / 2 complications / 2 surgical procedures.

2 complications
answer: Optic atrophy + corneal opacities → amblyopia (unilateral) or nystagmus/strabismus (bilateral) Subluxated lens — limbal enlargement weakens zonular ligaments

question:
Regarding Buphthalmos : 2 corneal signs / 2 complications / 2 surgical procedures.

2 surgical procedures + when corneal diameter >13 mm?
answer: Goniotomy — clear cornea, diameter Trabeculotomy — hazy cornea or goniotomy failure; trabeculotome via Schlemm's canal Corneal diameter >13 mm or both primary procedures fail → Subscleral trabeculectomy or Ahmed valve implantation

question:
56-year-old lady — severe headache, vomiting, "eye about to explode". VA left = HM. Stony-hard, edematous cornea, shallow AC, mid-dilated fixed pupil. Watched movie in cinema 3h prior.

Diagnosis
answer: Acute Primary Angle-Closure Glaucoma (Acute Congestive Glaucoma) — left eye

question:
56-year-old lady — severe headache, vomiting, "eye about to explode". VA left = HM. Stony-hard, edematous cornea, shallow AC, mid-dilated fixed pupil. Watched movie in cinema 3h prior.

Emergency measures to lower IOP
answer: IV Acetazolamide 500 mg — CAI, reduces aqueous secretion IV Mannitol 20% (1 g/kg) — osmotic agent, draws fluid from eye into circulation; or oral glycerol 50% Topical beta-blocker (timolol 0.5%) — reduces aqueous production Topical alpha-2 agonist (apraclonidine 1%) + topical CAI (brinzolamide) Pilocarpine 2–4% every 15 min — miotic, pulls iris from angle. Also apply to fellow eye prophylactically. Analgesics + antiemetics — severe pain/vomiting worsen Valsalva effect

question:
56-year-old lady — severe headache, vomiting, "eye about to explode". VA left = HM. Stony-hard, edematous cornea, shallow AC, mid-dilated fixed pupil. Watched movie in cinema 3h prior.

Definitive operative procedures
answer: Laser peripheral iridotomy (Nd:YAG) — first-line; hole in peripheral iris bypasses pupillary block; also done prophylactically on fellow eye Surgical peripheral iridectomy — if laser not feasible (persistent corneal edema) Trabeculectomy — if IOP control inadequate after laser/surgery

question:
Acute angle-closure glaucoma — diffuse corneal haziness. Expected findings on: AC depth / pupil diameter / IOP. Management choice?

Expected findings
answer: Finding Expected Result in Acute ACG Anterior chamber depth Very shallow — peripheral iris apposed to trabecular meshwork; iris bombé Pupil diameter Semi-dilated, vertically oval, fixed (non-reactive) — iris sphincter ischemic from elevated IOP IOP Markedly elevated — typically 40–80 mmHg; causes pain + corneal edema + optic nerve ischemia

question:
Acute angle-closure glaucoma — diffuse corneal haziness. Expected findings on: AC depth / pupil diameter / IOP. Management choice?

Correct management choice?
answer: ✅ C) Telephone request to an ophthalmologist for IMMEDIATE examination

question: Compare: Primary / Secondary / Consecutive / Post-glaucomatous optic atrophy — Color / Edge / Cup / Vessels / Retina.
answer: Feature Primary Secondary Consecutive Post-glaucomatous Color Chalk white Dirty grayish-white Waxy pale yellow Pale (cupped) Edge Sharp, well-defined Blurred, ill-defined Sharp Sharp (cup edge demarcated) Cup Normal Obliterated (glial tissue) Normal Markedly enlarged (C/D ≥ 0.7) Vessels Normal caliber Normal / slightly attenuated Markedly attenuated (thread-like) Displaced nasally; bayoneting Retina Normal Signs of cause (papilledema remnants) Pigmentary degeneration (RP/vascular) Normal outside disc Cause MS, tumor, trauma (no prior disc swelling) Follows papilledema/papillitis Follows retinal disease (RP, CRAO) Chronic raised IOP

question:
30-year-old female — rapid onset blurring + dull pain to eyebrow 2 days. VA left 6/18 (no PH improvement). IOP: R 16 / L 30 mmHg. Left: KPs on cornea, deep turbid AC, narrow miotic sluggish pupil.

Diagnosis
answer: Acute iridocyclitis (anterior uveitis) of the left eye + secondary glaucoma

question:
30-year-old female — rapid onset blurring + dull pain to eyebrow 2 days. VA left 6/18 (no PH improvement). IOP: R 16 / L 30 mmHg. Left: KPs on cornea, deep turbid AC, narrow miotic sluggish pupil.

4 probable causes of diminution of vision
answer: Aqueous and vitreous turbidity — inflammatory cells + protein reduce media transparency Corneal edema + KPs — reduce optical clarity Toxic macular edema (CME) — inflammatory mediators reach macula Secondary glaucoma (IOP 30 mmHg) — reduces retinal perfusion + optic nerve ischemia

question:
30-year-old female — rapid onset blurring + dull pain to eyebrow 2 days. VA left 6/18 (no PH improvement). IOP: R 16 / L 30 mmHg. Left: KPs on cornea, deep turbid AC, narrow miotic sluggish pupil.

4 possible complications
answer: Posterior synechiae → seclusio pupillae → iris bombé → secondary ACG Secondary glaucoma — early: plasmoid aqueous; late: PAS or pupillary block Complicated cataract — posterior cortical cataract from diffusion of inflammatory toxins Band keratopathy — calcium deposition in Bowman's layer (chronic/recurrent uveitis in children) Also accepted: Cyclitic membrane, phthisis bulbi, occlusio pupillae

question:
30-year-old female — rapid onset blurring + dull pain to eyebrow 2 days. VA left 6/18 (no PH improvement). IOP: R 16 / L 30 mmHg. Left: KPs on cornea, deep turbid AC, narrow miotic sluggish pupil.

4 lines of treatment
answer: Treat underlying cause — identify + remove septic foci (dental/tonsil/sinus); manage systemic disease Atropine 1% (mydriatic-cycloplegic) — dilates pupil, prevents/breaks synechiae, rests anterior uvea, relieves pain Topical corticosteroids — reduce exudation + synechiae; systemic steroids for severe/resistant cases Treat secondary glaucoma — oral acetazolamide + topical beta-blocker to lower IOP

question: Enumerate 6 complications of acute anterior uveitis .
answer: Posterior synechiae — iris-lens adhesions → festooned pupil, iris bombé, secondary ACG Secondary glaucoma — early (plasmoid aqueous) or late (PAS / pupillary block) Complicated cataract — posterior cortical from inflammatory toxins Cyclitic membrane — organized exudates behind lens; contraction → retinal detachment Band-shaped keratopathy — calcium in Bowman's layer (chronic/childhood uveitis) Hypotony + Phthisis bulbi — ciliary body atrophy → reduced aqueous → shrinkage of globe

question:
Regarding latent squint (heterophoria) : expected results in Hirschberg / Ocular motility / Cover-uncover test.

Hirschberg test
answer: Normal/Negative — central corneal reflexes symmetrically placed in BOTH pupils. Hirschberg only detects manifest (tropia), not latent, deviation. In heterophoria both eyes align binocularly when fixing on the light.

question:
Regarding latent squint (heterophoria) : expected results in Hirschberg / Ocular motility / Cover-uncover test.

Ocular motility
answer: Full and normal in all directions — no muscle paralysis/paresis in heterophoria. Deviation only manifests when fusion is disrupted.

question:
Regarding latent squint (heterophoria) : expected results in Hirschberg / Ocular motility / Cover-uncover test.

Cover-uncover test
answer: When eye is covered → covered eye moves under the cover (inward in esophoria, outward in exophoria). On removing cover → eye returns to aligned position (fusion restored). Uncovered (fixing) eye does NOT move. Movement under cover that corrects on uncovering = latent squint (heterophoria) . Distinguished from manifest squint where the uncovered eye moves to take fixation.

question: Name the test in the photo — finding seen — ocular part examined.
answer: Test name: Cover-Uncover Test (Cover Test) Finding: Movement of the uncovered eye to take up fixation when fellow eye is covered → manifest squint (heterotropia). OR: eye behind cover moves when cover removed → latent squint (heterophoria). Ocular part: Binocular alignment — visual axes and fixation behavior; assesses extraocular muscle coordination; differentiates heterophoria from heterotropia and paralytic from concomitant squint.

question:
55-year-old woman — acute right vision drop. Fundus: hemorrhage all over (right). Left: arterial attenuation + AV crossing changes. History of DM + HTN .

Probable diagnosis
answer: Central Retinal Vein Occlusion (CRVO) — right eye

question:
55-year-old woman — acute right vision drop. Fundus: hemorrhage all over (right). Left: arterial attenuation + AV crossing changes. History of DM + HTN .

Potential causes (enumerate)
answer: Hypertension (most common) — arteriosclerotic changes at lamina cribrosa compress CRV Diabetes mellitus — endothelial dysfunction + hypercoagulability Hyperlipidemia / Arteriosclerosis Glaucoma (raised IOP) — compresses CRV at optic disc Hypercoagulable states — polycythemia, thrombocytosis, multiple myeloma Vasculitis — sarcoidosis, Behçet's disease Oral contraceptive pills (young women) Dehydration — increases blood viscosity

question:
55-year-old woman — acute right vision drop. Fundus: hemorrhage all over (right). Left: arterial attenuation + AV crossing changes. History of DM + HTN .

Lines of treatment
answer: Treat underlying causes — control HTN, DM, hyperlipidemia Anti-VEGF intravitreal injections (ranibizumab, bevacizumab, aflibercept) — first-line for macular edema (main cause of VA loss) Intravitreal corticosteroids (triamcinolone, dexamethasone implant) — for anti-VEGF-unresponsive ME Macular grid laser — persistent macular edema Panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) — mandatory for ischemic CRVO with neovascularization Anticoagulation — selected cases with hypercoagulable states Treatment of neovascular glaucoma if it develops

question:
Regarding rhegmatogenous retinal detachment : 2 risk factors / 2 complications / 2 surgical procedures.

2 risk factors
answer: High myopia — longer axial length + thinner, degenerated peripheral retina prone to breaks Cataract surgery with vitreous loss — vitreous disruption → vitreoretinal traction → retinal tears Also accepted: Severe trauma, prior RD in fellow eye, family history

question:
Regarding rhegmatogenous retinal detachment : 2 risk factors / 2 complications / 2 surgical procedures.

2 complications
answer: Hypotony — increased fluid through break + reduced aqueous from ciliary traction Permanent visual loss — "macula-off" detachment → prolonged ischemia of photoreceptors → irreversible damage

question:
Regarding rhegmatogenous retinal detachment : 2 risk factors / 2 complications / 2 surgical procedures.

2 surgical procedures
answer: Scleral buckling + cryotherapy — silicone band sutured around sclera to indent eye wall, closing the break; cryotherapy creates chorioretinal adhesion Pars plana vitrectomy + endo-laser — internal approach; removes vitreous gel + photocoagulation around retinal tear to seal it

question:
Regarding diabetic retinopathy : signs in NPDR / causes of vision loss / indications for vitrectomy.

2 retinal signs in NPDR stage
answer: Microaneurysms — earliest sign; small saccular outpouchings of retinal capillaries → tiny red dots on fundoscopy Dot-blot hemorrhages — intraretinal hemorrhages (inner nuclear/outer nuclear layer) from microaneurysm rupture Also accepted: Hard exudates, cotton-wool spots, venous changes

question:
Regarding diabetic retinopathy : signs in NPDR / causes of vision loss / indications for vitrectomy.

2 causes of diminution of vision in DR
answer: Diabetic macular edema (DME) — most common; leakage from microaneurysms → fluid in macula → disrupts foveal photoreceptors (any stage) Vitreous hemorrhage — PDR: fragile new vessels rupture → blood in vitreous → sudden painless severe visual loss Others: Tractional RD, neovascular glaucoma, macular ischemia

question:
Regarding diabetic retinopathy : signs in NPDR / causes of vision loss / indications for vitrectomy.

2 indications for vitreoretinal surgery (PPV) in PDR
answer: Non-clearing vitreous hemorrhage — persistent blood >3 months preventing laser + causing significant visual loss Tractional RD involving/threatening the macula — fibrovascular membranes contracting over macula → severe VA loss Also accepted: Combined tractional + rhegmatogenous RD; dense premacular hemorrhage

question: Illustrate a diagram for cross-section of the retina demonstrating its 10 layers .
answer: # Layer Clinical note 1 Internal Limiting Membrane (ILM) Basement membrane of Müller cells 2 Nerve Fiber Layer (NFL) Axons of RGCs → optic nerve; flame hemorrhages here 3 Ganglion Cell Layer (GCL) Cell bodies of RGCs 4 Inner Plexiform Layer (IPL) Bipolar → ganglion synapses 5 Inner Nuclear Layer (INL) Bipolar, amacrine, Müller cells; dot-blot hemorrhages 6 Outer Plexiform Layer (OPL) Photoreceptor → bipolar synapses 7 Outer Nuclear Layer (ONL) Photoreceptor cell bodies 8 External Limiting Membrane (ELM) Müller cell junctional complexes 9 Photoreceptor Layer (Rods & Cones) Phototransduction occurs here 10 Retinal Pigment Epithelium (RPE) Separated from choroid by Bruch's membrane ⭐ Retinal detachment occurs between layer 9 (neuroretina) and layer 10 (RPE) — the potential space of the embryonic optic cup.

question:
Photo — vesicular rash, dermatomal distribution, forehead/periorbital region, nose tip involvement. Herpes Zoster Ophthalmicus questions.

Diagnosis

Photo — vesicular rash, dermatomal distribution, forehead/periorbital region, nose tip involvement. Herpes Zoster Ophthalmicus questions.

Name of sign (lesion on nose tip)
answer: Hutchinson's sign — vesicular lesions on the tip/side of the nose → involvement of the nasociliary branch of V1 → predicts higher risk of ocular involvement.

question:
Photo — vesicular rash, dermatomal distribution, forehead/periorbital region, nose tip involvement. Herpes Zoster Ophthalmicus questions.

Nerve involved
answer: Ophthalmic division (V1) of the trigeminal nerve (CN V) — includes frontal, lacrimal, and nasociliary branches → unilateral dermatomal distribution over forehead, upper eyelid, periorbital region.

answer: Disciform keratitis: Immune-mediated (hypersensitivity) reaction to HSV/VZV antigens in the corneal stroma — NOT active viral replication in the stroma.

question: Sling operation should be avoided in cases of ptosis with
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Ophthalmology 43 G2 (Batch 1)
explain: The frontalis suspension (sling/Hiss) operation is the preferred surgical technique when levator function is poor (≤4 mm). However, it is specifically contraindicated when the patient has absent or poor Bell's phenomenon. Bell's phenomenon is the normal upward and outward rolling of the eye during attempted lid closure, which protects the cornea when the lids are open. If the sling operation is performed in a patient without this protective mechanism, the elevated lid will expose the cornea postoperatively without the safety net of the upward eye roll, leading to exposure keratitis and corneal ulceration. Poor levator function is actually the main indication, not a contraindication, for the sling operation.

question: Regarding the tarsal plates, all the following is true except
options: Of the upper lid are about 10 mm in height
Of the lower lid are about 4 mm in height
Impart structural integrity to the eyelids
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Ophthalmology 43 G2 (Batch 1)
explain: The tarsal plates are dense fibrous tissue structures that form the structural skeleton of the eyelids. The upper tarsus is 8–12 mm in height; the lower tarsus is approximately 4–5 mm in height. They contain the Meibomian glands (tarsal/sebaceous glands), not Moll's glands. Moll's glands are modified sweat glands located at the lid margin near the lash roots, not embedded in the tarsal plate. Lash follicles are also in the anterior lamella, not in the tarsus itself.

question: An 11-year-old patient presents with a variable position of the left upper eyelid. The observed changes occur with opening and closing of the mouth. The condition is most commonly due to synkinesis of which two cranial nerves?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Ophthalmology 43 G2 (Batch 1)
explain: The clinical scenario describes Marcus Gunn jaw-winking phenomenon — a form of congenital synkinesis where the ptosed eyelid elevates when the patient opens or moves the jaw (chewing, mouth opening). This is due to an aberrant connection between the motor root of the trigeminal nerve (CN V — innervating the pterygoid muscles of mastication) and the oculomotor nerve (CN III — innervating the levator palpebrae superioris). As jaw movement triggers CN V activity, a misfired signal simultaneously innervates the levator, elevating the ptosed lid.

question: A 4-year-old boy presented with left sided ptosis since birth. Best corrected VA is 6/6 in right eye and 6/18 in left eye. MRD1 is 5 mm right and -1 mm left. Levator function is 15 mm right and 3 mm left. There is no lagophthalmos. Which of the following would be your recommendation?
options: Observation Till The Age Of 5 Years
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Ophthalmology 43 G2 (Batch 1)
explain: This child has severe congenital ptosis (MRD1 of -1 mm, meaning the lid covers the pupil entirely) with very poor levator function (3 mm, which is classified as poor, ≤4 mm). According to the textbook, when levator function is poor and ptosis is severe, the surgical treatment of choice is frontalis suspension (sling/Hiss operation), which uses the frontalis muscle to elevate the lid. There is also amblyopia in the affected eye (VA 6/18), making early surgery important. Resection/advancement of the levator is only indicated when levator function is fair to good (≥5 mm). Muller's muscle resection is used for mild ptosis with good levator function.

question: A 32-year-old female patient was complaining from redness in her eyelids with frequent falling of lashes. On examination eyelids were thickened, hyperemic, with lashes glued together and irregularities in the lid margin. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Ophthalmology 43 G2 (Batch 1)
explain: The clinical picture described — thickened, hyperemic lid margins, lashes matted and glued together with crusts, irregular lid margin, and madarosis (loss/falling of lashes) — is characteristic of ulcerative (anterior) blepharitis. It is caused by Staphylococcal infection of the lash follicles and their associated glands (Zeis and Moll). The ulceration of lash follicles leads to scarring and permanent lash loss (madarosis), irregular lid margins, and distorted lashes (trichiasis). In contrast, scaly (seborrhoeic) blepharitis shows greasy scales without true ulceration and does not typically cause lash loss.

question: Male infant with mild ptosis, anisocoria, heterochromia, normal ocular motility. Which muscle is affected?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Ophthalmology 43 G2 (Batch 1)
explain: The combination of mild ptosis, miosis (small pupil causing anisocoria), and heterochromia iridis in a child is the classic triad of congenital Horner's syndrome. In Horner's syndrome, the sympathetic innervation to the eye is disrupted. The sympathetic system innervates Müller's muscle (superior tarsal muscle) in the upper lid, the inferior tarsal muscle in the lower lid, the dilator pupillae, and the sweat glands of the face. The mild ptosis (2–3 mm) of Horner's syndrome is due specifically to paralysis of Müller's muscle — not the levator palpebrae superioris (which is innervated by CN III). Heterochromia (lighter iris on the affected side) occurs in congenital cases due to lack of sympathetic stimulation for iris pigmentation.

question: 52-year-old patient with recurrent chalazion (three times) in the upper eyelid should be managed by
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Ophthalmology 43 G2 (Batch 1)
explain: The textbook explicitly states: "Recurrent swelling in the same gland: Excision biopsy and histopathological examination is done to exclude malignancy." A chalazion recurring three times in the same meibomian gland is a red flag for meibomian gland carcinoma (sebaceous gland adenocarcinoma), which can mimic a chalazion clinically. This malignancy carries a significant risk of regional and systemic spread. Therefore, any recurrent chalazion in the same location must be excised completely and submitted for histopathological examination to exclude malignancy, not simply curetted or treated with antibiotics.

question: A 8-year-old boy has bilateral ptosis since birth, worsening over time, with normal milestones and no birth trauma. What is the cause of this condition?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Ophthalmology 43 G2 (Batch 1)
explain: Bilateral congenital ptosis that is present since birth, progressive, and not associated with trauma, systemic disease, or neurological findings in an otherwise healthy child is caused by levator muscle dysgenesis — faulty development of the levator palpebrae superioris muscle or its nerve supply. The textbook states that congenital ptosis is due to "faulty development of the levator muscle or its nerve supply (levator muscle dysgenesis leading to poor contraction and incomplete relaxation)." It is dated since birth and usually bilateral. Oculomotor nerve palsy would be unilateral and associated with extraocular muscle involvement and pupil dilation. Facial nerve palsy causes lagophthalmos and ectropion, not ptosis.

question: A 32 year old female patient was complaining from redness in her eyelids with frequent falling of lashes. On examinations eye lids were thickened, hyperemic, with lashes glued together and irregularities in the lid margin. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from END 41 G2 (Batch 2)
explain: The clinical picture describes ulcerative (anterior) blepharitis caused by *Staphylococcus aureus*. The hallmark features are thickened, hyperemic lid margins with lashes glued together by yellow crusts; when these crusts are removed, minute bleeding ulcers are seen at the lid margin. Frequent falling of lashes (madarosis) is a recognized complication due to destruction of hair follicles by the chronic infection. Scaly blepharitis shows greasy scales without ulceration; angular blepharitis affects the canthi; allergic blepharitis is characterized by itching and chemosis rather than ulceration.

question: Regarding causes of lagophthalmos, which of the following causes is wrong?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from END 41 G2 (Batch 2)
explain: Lagophthalmos is incomplete closure of the palpebral fissure. Its established causes include thyroid exophthalmos (proptosis prevents full lid closure), lower motor neuron facial palsy (paralysis of orbicularis oculi), lid coloboma (structural loss of lid tissue), overcorrection of ptosis, buphthalmos, and comatose states. Severe entropion (inward rolling of the lid margin) does not cause lagophthalmos; in fact, it causes the opposite problem — the lid margin turns inward, and the lashes rub against the cornea. It is severe ectropion or post-operative overcorrection that can contribute to incomplete lid closure.

question: Regarding the tarsal plates, all the following is true except
options: Of the upper lid are about 10 mm in height
Of the lower lid are about 4 mm in height
Impart structural integrity to the eyelids
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from END 41 G2 (Batch 2)
explain: The tarsal plates are dense fibrous tissue structures that form the structural skeleton of the eyelids. The upper tarsus is 8–12 mm in height; the lower tarsus is approximately 4–5 mm in height. They contain the Meibomian (tarsal) glands, not Moll's glands. Moll's glands are modified sweat glands located at the eyelid margin, associated with the eyelash follicles. The tarsal plates do not contain lash follicles and do impart structural integrity to the lids. Therefore, the statement that the tarsal plates contain Moll's glands is false.

question: Male infant presented with left mild ptosis with anisocoria & heterochromia iridis with normal ocular motility. Which one of the following eyelid muscles is affected in this patient?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from END 41 G2 (Batch 2)
explain: The triad of mild ptosis, miosis (smaller pupil = anisocoria), and heterochromia iridis (lighter iris color on the affected side in congenital cases) with normal ocular motility is the classic presentation of Horner's syndrome. In Horner's syndrome, the sympathetic supply to the eye is disrupted, affecting: (1) Müller's muscle in the upper lid (causing mild ptosis of 1–2 mm), (2) the dilator pupillae muscle (causing miosis), and (3) melanocyte stimulation (causing heterochromia iridis in congenital cases). Ocular motility is normal because the extraocular muscles and levator (innervated by cranial nerve III) are unaffected. Horner's muscle (pars lacrimalis of orbicularis) is responsible for the lacrimal pump, not ptosis.

question: A 32 year old female patient was complaining from redness in her eyelids with frequent falling of lashes. On examinations eye lids were thickened, hyperemic, with lashes glued together and irregularities in the lid margin. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Module 41 G2 (Batch 3)
explain: The clinical picture — thickened, hyperemic lid margins with lashes glued together by crusts and lid margin irregularities with frequent lash loss (madarosis) — is characteristic of ulcerative (anterior) blepharitis. The textbook describes ulcerative blepharitis: "In ulcerative type, lashes are glued together by yellow crusts and when the crusts are removed, they leave minute bleeding ulcers at the margin." Complications include "Madarosis: due to destruction of hair follicles." Scaly blepharitis shows greasy scales between lashes without bleeding ulcers. Angular blepharitis affects the lateral canthus. Allergic blepharitis presents with itch and lid swelling, not ulcerated margins.

question: Regarding causes of lagophthalmos, which of the following causes is wrong?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Module 41 G2 (Batch 3)
explain: Severe entropion causes the lid margin to roll inward, which results in lashes rubbing against the globe — it does not cause lagophthalmos (inability to close the eyelids). The textbook lists causes of lagophthalmos under "Local causes — Pathological" including severe ectropion (not entropion), structural loss (coloboma), postoperative cases, spastic lid retraction (thyroid ophthalmopathy), and lower motor neuron facial palsy. Comatose patients develop physiological/pathological lagophthalmos. Severe entropion is not a cause.

question: Regarding the tarsal plates, all the following is true except
options: Of the upper lid are about 10 mm in height
Of the lower lid are about 4 mm in height
Impart structural integrity to the eyelids
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Module 41 G2 (Batch 3)
explain: The tarsal plates contain the Meibomian (tarsal) glands, not Moll's glands. The textbook states: "The tarsal plates consist of dense fibrous tissue forming the skeleton of the lids. The upper tarsus height is 8-12 mm, the lower one is about 4-5 mm." And: "The meibomian (tarsal) glands: They are elongated sebaceous glands embedded within the tarsal plate." Moll's glands are modified sweat glands located at the lid margin near the lash roots, not embedded in the tarsal plate. Lash follicles are also in the anterior lamella, not in the tarsus itself.

question: Male infant presented with left mild ptosis with anisocoria & heterochromia iridis with normal ocular motility. Which one of the following eyelid muscles is affected in this patient?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End Module 41 G2 (Batch 3)
explain: The combination of mild ptosis, miosis (anisocoria with a smaller pupil on the affected side), and heterochromia iridis with normal ocular motility is classic for Horner's syndrome. Müller's muscle (superior tarsal muscle) is the sympathetically innervated smooth muscle of the upper eyelid. Its dysfunction results in mild ptosis (1-2 mm, less than that of levator paralysis). The textbook states: "Müller's muscle: Action: to sustain elevation of the upper eyelid... It's innervated by autonomic sympathetic nerves, so its paralysis will lead to Horner's syndrome." The dilator pupillae is also sympathetically innervated (causing miosis), and in congenital cases, heterochromia iridis occurs due to failure of iris pigmentation.

question: A patient presenting with a swelling in his upper lid for one year that does not change in size and is now becoming red and painful. What is first line management?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from 41 End Module (Batch 4)
explain: The clinical scenario describes a chronic chalazion (lipogranuloma of the meibomian gland lasting one year) that has now become acutely infected — i.e., an infected chalazion (hordeolum internum). When a chalazion becomes acutely inflamed and painful, the first-line management is treatment of the infection with systemic and local antibiotics, as is the approach for hordeolum internum. Simple incision and curettage are reserved for large, non-infected chalazia. Histopathological excision is indicated for recurrent chalazia in the same gland to exclude meibomian gland carcinoma — not for first-time infected presentations.

question: A 62-year-old woman reports that her right eye has been irritated, red, tearing for months. Examination revealed rolled in lower lid margin. Which one of the following is least likely to be the cause of this condition?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from 41 End Module (Batch 4)
explain: The condition described is entropion (inward rolling of the lid margin). The recognized etiological types of entropion are: congenital, cicatricial, spastic, and senile (involutional). Paralytic entropion is not a recognized category — paralysis of the facial nerve (7th nerve) causes paralytic ECTROPION (outward rolling of the lid) due to loss of orbicularis tone, not entropion. Therefore, "paralytic" is the least likely cause of entropion in this patient.

question: Male patient presented with drooping of the right upper eyelid, which is corrected on covering the left eye. What is the most probable diagnosis?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from 41 End Module (Batch 4)
explain: When ptosis of one eye is "corrected" upon covering the opposite eye, this strongly suggests that the apparent ptosis is actually pseudoptosis due to contralateral lid retraction. According to Hering's law of equal innervation, both levator muscles receive equal neural input. If one eye has true lid retraction (requiring extra levator innervation), the fellow levator also receives excess innervation and holds the lid at a normal or even low position. When the retracted eye is covered, the excessive innervation is removed, and the "ptotic" eye assumes a normal lid position. Uncovering the opposite eye restores the pseudo-ptosis.

question: Female patient with cicatricial ectropion of the lower lid secondary to large cutaneous scar. Which of the following is the probable treatment?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from 41 End Module (Batch 4)
explain: Cicatricial ectropion results from scarring of the anterior lamella (skin) of the eyelid, causing vertical shortening and outward rotation of the lid margin. The management depends on the size of the scar: for small scars, a V-Y plasty or Z-plasty can lengthen the contracted tissue. However, for LARGE cutaneous scars causing cicatricial ectropion, the treatment requires excision of the scar tissue followed by skin grafting to replace the lost tissue and restore normal lid position. Snellen's sutures are used for senile (involutional) ectropion, not cicatricial ectropion.

question: Female patient with cicatricial ectropion of the lower lid secondary to large cutaneous scar. Which of the following is the probable treatment?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from 41 End Module (Batch 4)
explain: Cicatricial ectropion results from scarring of the anterior lamella (skin) of the eyelid, causing vertical shortening and outward rotation of the lid margin. For LARGE cutaneous scars causing cicatricial ectropion, the treatment requires excision of the scar tissue followed by skin grafting to replace the lost tissue and restore normal lid position. Snellen's sutures are used for senile (involutional) ectropion, not cicatricial ectropion.

question: An 11-year-old patient presents with a variable position of the left upper eyelid. The observed changes occur with opening and closing of the mouth. The condition is most commonly due to synkinesis of which two cranial nerves?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from END module 40 (Batch 5)
explain: The described condition is Marcus Gunn jaw-winking phenomenon — a congenital ptosis in which the ptosed upper eyelid elevates (winks) on opening the jaw or moving the mandible. It is caused by faulty innervation: an aberrant synkinesis between the motor branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V, supplying the external pterygoid jaw muscle) and the branch of the oculomotor nerve (CN III) supplying the levator palpebrae superioris. When the pterygoid muscle contracts during jaw movement, an anomalous neural connection causes simultaneous contraction of the levator, elevating the lid.

question: A 62-year-old woman reports that her right eye has been irritated, red, tearing for months. Examination revealed rolled in lower lid margin. Which one of the following is LEAST likely to be the cause of this condition?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from END module 40 (Batch 5)
explain: The described condition is entropion — inward rolling of the lower lid margin. The recognized etiological types of entropion are: involutional/senile (most common; due to horizontal lid laxity and overriding of the preseptal orbicularis), spastic (due to blepharospasm from ocular irritation), and cicatricial (due to conjunctival scarring from conditions such as trachoma or chemical burns). Paralytic entropion is not a recognized entity. In facial nerve paralysis, orbicularis muscle weakness causes ectropion (outward rolling), not entropion. Therefore, "paralytic" is the least likely cause of entropion.

question: Regarding the tarsal plates, all the following is true EXCEPT
options: Of the upper lid are about 10 mm in height
Of the lower lid are about 4 mm in height
Impart structural integrity to the eyelids
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from END module 40 (Batch 5)
explain: The tarsal plates are dense fibrous structures forming the skeleton of the eyelids, imparting structural integrity. Their heights are approximately 8–12 mm (upper tarsus) and 4–5 mm (lower tarsus). Embedded within the tarsal plates are the meibomian (tarsal) glands — elongated sebaceous glands whose orifices open at the lid margin behind the grey line. The Moll's glands (modified sweat glands) are located at the anterior eyelid margin near the eyelash follicles — they are related to the lashes, NOT embedded within the tarsus. The Zeiss glands (sebaceous glands related to lash follicles) are similarly located at the lid margin rather than within the tarsal plate.

question: A 4-year-old boy with left-sided ptosis since birth. Best corrected visual acuity was 6/6 right and 6/18 left. MRD 1 is 5 mm right and -1 mm left. Levator function is 15 mm right and 3 mm left. No lagophthalmos. Which of the following would be your recommendation?
options: Observation till the age of 5 years
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End 40 G1 (Batch 6)
explain: This child has severe congenital ptosis with very poor levator function (only 3 mm — classified as poor: <4 mm) and MRD1 of -1 mm (the lid completely covers the pupil). The reduced visual acuity (6/18) indicates amblyopia from visual deprivation by the drooping lid. Because levator function is severely limited, levator resection/advancement would be ineffective. The treatment of choice is frontalis suspension (sling operation), which connects the tarsal plate to the frontalis muscle, allowing the patient to use brow elevation to lift the eyelid. Prompt surgery is indicated to treat amblyopia.

question: Female patient with cicatricial ectropion of the lower lid secondary to large cutaneous scar. Which of the following is the probable treatment?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End 40 G1 (Batch 6)
explain: Cicatricial ectropion results from vertical skin shortage due to scarring of the anterior lamella (skin and orbicularis). Treatment requires replacing the deficit with additional tissue. For large cutaneous scars causing significant ectropion, free skin grafting (full-thickness or split-thickness) is the treatment of choice — it provides the needed extra skin to relieve vertical tension and allow the eyelid to return to its normal position. V-Y plasty addresses small areas; tarsorrhaphy addresses exposure but not the cicatricial mechanism; Snellen's suture corrects senile (involutional) ectropion.

question: Male patient presented with drooping of the right upper eyelid, which is corrected on covering the left eye. What is the probable diagnosis?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End 40 G1 (Batch 6)
explain: When apparent ptosis of one eye is corrected by covering the other eye, this is pseudoptosis due to Hering's Law of equal innervation. Contralateral hypertropia (the left eye is deviated upward) triggers extra innervation to the left superior rectus. By Hering's Law, the paired antagonist (right levator — via bilateral equal innervation) receives increased innervation to maintain fixation. When the left eye is covered, this extra stimulus is removed, and the right lid elevation normalizes, correcting the apparent "ptosis." This is a classical clinical sign of pseudoptosis from contralateral hypertropia.

question: Sling operation should be avoided in cases of ptosis with
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from End 40 G1 (Batch 6)
explain: Frontalis sling (suspension) operation for ptosis keeps the eye partially open even at rest, relying on corneal protection primarily from Bell's phenomenon — the normal reflex upward rotation of the globe on attempted lid closure, which protects the cornea when the lid cannot close completely. If Bell's phenomenon is poor or absent, sling surgery would expose the cornea to serious risk of exposure keratopathy, desiccation, and corneal ulceration during sleep. Poor Bell's phenomenon is therefore a relative contraindication for frontalis sling surgery. Very poor levator function, severe ptosis, and multiple failed surgeries are actually indications for the sling procedure.

question: On swabbing crusts from a patient with ulcerative blepharitis, what is the organism that will be found?
explain: Ulcerative (anterior) blepharitis is caused by Staphylococcus aureus infection of the eyelid margin. The crusts seen in this type of blepharitis are yellow (not greasy/white as in seborrheic blepharitis), and when removed, they reveal minute bleeding ulcers at the lid margin. Swabbing these crusts cultures Staphylococcus aureus. Morax-Axenfeld diplobacillus causes angular blepharoconjunctivitis (affecting the canthal angles). Pneumococcus is associated with corneal ulcers (hypopyon ulcer). E. coli is not a typical cause of blepharitis.

question: A fifty years-old male with sudden onset of 7th nerve palsy affecting all the facial muscles. What is your first line of treatment?
options: Immediate resection of the levator muscle
explain: In acute facial (7th nerve) palsy, orbicularis oculi paralysis causes lagophthalmos (inability to close the eyelids), exposing the cornea to drying and ulceration. The immediate and first-line treatment is continuous ocular lubrication — artificial tear drops during the day and lubricating ointment at night, combined with protective glasses and eyelid taping during sleep. This protects the cornea while awaiting possible nerve regeneration (which may occur within 6 months). Lateral tarsorrhaphy is reserved for severe or permanent cases unresponsive to lubrication. Levator resection would worsen lagophthalmos. Topical corticosteroids alone do not protect the cornea from exposure.

question: A patient complaining of foreign body sensation was found to have 2 mal-directed lashes in the middle of his upper eyelid. The lid margin was otherwise normal. The best treatment is
explain: The described condition is trichiasis — misdirected eyelashes rubbing against the corneal surface, causing a foreign body sensation. When only a few (1–2) isolated lashes are misdirected and the lid margin is otherwise normal (ruling out diffuse cicatricial entropion), the treatment of choice for these individual aberrant lashes is electrolysis. Electrolysis permanently destroys the hair follicle using electrical current, preventing regrowth of the offending lash. Snellen's operation and Van Millingen's operation are surgical procedures for correcting entropion (inward rotation of the entire lid margin) — they are appropriate for widespread trichiasis secondary to entropion, not for a few isolated misdirected lashes with an otherwise normal lid.

question: A 32 female patient complaining from redness in her eyelids with frequent falling of lashes. On examinations eye lids were thickened, hyperemic, with lashes glued together and irregularities in the lid margin. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explain: Ulcerative (anterior) blepharitis is caused by Staphylococcus aureus infection of the lid margin. The hallmark signs are lashes glued together by yellow crusts, and when crusts are removed they leave minute bleeding ulcers. Lid margin thickening, hyperemia, and lash loss (madarosis) are classic features. Scaly blepharitis produces greasy grayish-white scales between lashes without ulceration, while parasitic and allergic types have different presentations.

question: A patient presenting with a swelling in his upper lid for one year that does not change in size and is now becoming red and painful. The first line management is
explain: A chronic non-tender lid swelling that has been present for one year and is now becoming red and painful indicates an infected chalazion (acute chalazion / hordeolum internum). The first line of treatment for an infected chalazion is systemic and local antibiotics plus hot fomentations to resolve the acute infection. Once the acute infection subsides, incision and curettage can be performed for the residual chalazion. Histopathology is indicated for recurrent chalazia to rule out meibomian gland carcinoma, not for acute presentations.

question: A 32 female patient complaining from redness in her eyelids with frequent falling of lashes. On examinations eye lids were thickened, hyperemic, with lashes glued together and irregularities in the lid margin. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Final 39 Summer (Batch 10)
explain: Ulcerative (anterior) blepharitis is caused by Staphylococcus aureus infection of the lid margin. The hallmark is lashes glued together by yellow crusts that, when removed, leave minute bleeding ulcers. Lid margin thickening, hyperemia, and lash loss (madarosis) are classic signs. Scaly blepharitis produces greasy grayish-white scales without ulcers; allergic blepharitis causes edema and itching; parasitic type is associated with Demodex infestation.

question: Regarding causes of lagophthalmos, which of the following causes is wrong?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Final 39 Summer (Batch 10)
explain: Lagophthalmos is the inability to completely close the eyelids, which exposes the cornea to desiccation and damage. Causes include thyroid exophthalmos (proptosis pushes the eye forward making closure inadequate), facial palsy (7th nerve — orbicularis oculi weakness prevents lid closure), lid coloboma (a congenital notch in the lid), and comatose patients (reduced Bell's reflex and absent voluntary closure). Severe entropion (D) is the inturning of the lid margin, which causes the lid to rub against the cornea, but does NOT cause lagophthalmos; rather it causes excess lid-to-globe contact — the opposite problem.

question: A 52-year-old patient with recurrent chalazion (three times) in the upper eyelid should be managed by
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Final 39 Summer (Batch 10)
explain: A chalazion that recurs multiple times in the same site must be excised and sent for histopathological examination. Recurrent chalazia in an elderly patient may represent a meibomian gland carcinoma (sebaceous cell carcinoma) masquerading as a chalazion — a malignant and potentially life-threatening condition. This is a critical clinical rule: any chalazion that recurs at the same site, especially in older patients, requires biopsy. Simple curettage without histology would miss a potential carcinoma.

question: Male infant presented with left mild ptosis with anisocoria and heterochromia iridis with normal ocular motility. Which one of the following eyelid muscles is affected in this patient?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Final 39 Summer (Batch 10)
explain: The triad of mild ptosis (1–2 mm), miosis (small pupil — causing anisocoria), and anhidrosis characterizes Horner's syndrome, caused by disruption of the sympathetic pathway. In the eyelid, the sympathetically innervated muscle is Müller's muscle (superior tarsal muscle), which contributes approximately 2 mm of upper eyelid elevation. Its loss causes the mild ptosis seen in Horner's syndrome. Heterochromia iridis (difference in iris color) can also occur, especially in congenital Horner's. The levator is supplied by CN III (causing complete ptosis when affected, not mild); Riolan's muscle keeps lid apposed to globe; Horner's muscle is the lacrimal part of orbicularis.

question: Which one of the following is linked to lesion of the fifth cranial nerve?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Final 40 Summer (Batch 11)
explain: Neurotrophic keratitis results from damage to the sensory nerve supply of the cornea, which is carried by the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve (5th cranial nerve). The textbook states: "It is a primary non-infective keratitis due to absence of corneal sensation" caused by "paralysis of the sensory nerve supply to the cornea which is carried by the ophthalmic division of the Trigeminal nerve." Ectropion is linked to 7th nerve palsy, lagophthalmos also to 7th nerve palsy, ptosis to 3rd nerve palsy, and proptosis to orbital disease.

question: A 19-year-old male with sudden onset of dropped upper lid and constriction of the pupil in right eye. What is the most likely diagnosis?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Final 40 Summer (Batch 11)
explain: The combination of unilateral ptosis and miosis (pupil constriction) in a young patient is classic for Horner's syndrome. The textbook describes Horner's syndrome as "sympathetic ptosis: mild unilateral ptosis, miosis and anhidrosis, enophthalmos, flushing of face and heterochromia iridis (in congenital cases)." This occurs because Müller's muscle (which sustains upper lid elevation) and the dilator pupillae are both sympathetically innervated, so their paralysis causes ptosis and miosis simultaneously. Third nerve palsy causes ptosis with mydriasis (dilated pupil), not miosis.

question: Female patient with cicatricial ectropion of the lower lid secondary to large cutaneous scar. Which of the following is the probable treatment?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Final 40 Summer (Batch 11)
explain: Cicatricial ectropion due to a large scar requires surgical correction. The textbook specifies: "Small scar: V-Y operation. Large scar: Z-plasty, or removal of the scar tissue followed by eyelid grafting (skin grafting)." Since the scar is described as large, skin grafting is the appropriate treatment. Snellen's sutures are used for moderate senile ectropion. Tarsorrhaphy is reserved for exposure keratopathy protection, not for correcting cicatricial ectropion.

question: Male patient presented with drooping of the right upper eyelid, which is corrected on covering the left eye. What is the probable diagnosis?
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Final 40 Summer (Batch 11)
explain: When drooping of one upper lid is corrected by covering the opposite eye, this suggests the ptosis is pseudo-ptosis caused by contralateral hypertropia (the opposite eye is elevated, causing the brain to send inhibitory signals to the ipsilateral eye, making the lid appear to droop). When the hypertropic eye is covered and forced to fixate, the ipsilateral eye takes over and the apparent ptosis resolves. This is a classic presentation of pseudoptosis due to contralateral hypertropia.

question: Sling operation should be avoided in cases of ptosis with
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Final 40 Summer (Batch 11)
explain: The frontalis sling (Hiss operation) is contraindicated in patients with poor Bell's phenomenon. The textbook lists contraindications to ptosis surgery including "Ptosis associated with neuroparalytic keratitis or absent Bell's phenomenon." Bell's phenomenon is the protective upward rotation of the eye on attempted closure. Without it, after a sling operation the cornea will be exposed and vulnerable to exposure keratopathy, because the patient cannot protect the eye when the lids are not fully closed. The sling is actually indicated (not contraindicated) for poor levator function and severe ptosis.

question: A recurrent chalazion should be subjected to histopathologic evaluation to exclude the possibility of
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Lid and Cornea Quiz (Batch 13)
explain: The textbook states that a "recurrent swelling in the same gland" warrants "excision biopsy and histopathological examination to exclude malignancy." Sebaceous cell carcinoma (adenocarcinoma of the Meibomian gland) is the most important differential diagnosis for a recurrent chalazion, as it can clinically mimic a chalazion and arises from the same sebaceous (Meibomian) glands. It is listed under the differential diagnosis of chalazion in the textbook.

question: Ptosis is seen in
options: Damage to motor part of facial nerve
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Lid and Cornea Quiz (Batch 13)
explain: The textbook describes Horner's syndrome as resulting from damage to the sympathetic nerve supply, producing "mild unilateral ptosis, miosis and anhidrosis." Müller's muscle, which contributes to upper eyelid elevation, is innervated by sympathetic nerves; its paralysis leads to ptosis. Damage to the Edinger-Westphal nucleus affects pupillary constriction, facial nerve damage causes lagophthalmos (inability to close the eye), and the lacrimal nerve carries sensation and lacrimation — none of which cause ptosis.

question: Which of the following conditions is the most painful
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Lid and Cornea Quiz (Batch 13)
explain: The textbook describes hordeolum internum (infected chalazion) as having "the same clinical picture as in stye but pain is more severe." This is because the Meibomian gland is larger and embedded within the tarsus, making pus accumulate under tension — leading to more intense pain. In contrast, chalazion is described as a "painless swelling," and stye (hordeolum externum) causes a painful red swelling but less severe than hordeolum internum.

question: Senile ptosis is
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Lid and Cornea Quiz (Batch 13)
explain: The textbook classifies senile (involutional) ptosis under the "Aponeurotic" type, describing it as "due to degenerative changes in levator muscle aponeurosis while the levator function is good." With age, the levator aponeurosis stretches, dehisces, or disinserts from the tarsus, causing the upper lid to droop despite an otherwise intact and functional levator muscle. Surgical repair involves levator aponeurosis repair or levator resection.

question: Sling operation should be avoided in cases of ptosis with
tags: Eye Lid: Questions from Lid and Cornea Quiz (Batch 13)
explain: The textbook lists "ptosis associated with neuroparalytic keratitis or absent Bell's phenomenon" as a contraindication to ptosis surgery, including the frontalis sling (Hiss operation). Bell's phenomenon is the protective upward rotation of the eyeball on lid closure. If it is absent or poor, surgical correction of ptosis risks corneal exposure and exposure keratitis, since the cornea will not be protected when the eyelid is elevated. Poor Bell's phenomenon is therefore a specific contraindication to the sling operation.

question: Epiphora following Vth nerve palsy may be due to?
options: Failure of the lacrimal pump system
tags: Lacrimal System: Questions from End Ophthalmology 43 G2 (Batch 1)
explain: Epiphora is defined as overflow of tears due to obstruction or failure of the lacrimal drainage system, with normal tear secretion. The 5th cranial nerve (trigeminal nerve) provides sensory innervation to the cornea and conjunctiva; its palsy results in corneal anesthesia and loss of the afferent limb of the corneal reflex. In 5th nerve palsy, tear secretion is not excessive; in fact, reflex secretion may be diminished due to loss of sensory stimulation. Lower eyelid entropion is not caused by 5th nerve palsy (that is caused by 7th nerve palsy causing ectropion). The lacrimal pump requires orbicularis oculi function (7th nerve), not the 5th nerve. Therefore, epiphora is not a direct consequence of 5th nerve palsy; none of the listed mechanisms apply.

question: A 45-year-old female with chronic dacryocystitis. Which of the following most likely describes her condition?
options: Swelling below the medial palpebral ligament
answer: Swelling below the medial palpebral ligament
tags: Lacrimal System: Questions from End 40 G1 (Batch 2)
explain: Chronic dacryocystitis (infection of the lacrimal sac) presents with a painless swelling in the medial canthal area below the medial palpebral ligament (the lacrimal sac lies below this structure, whereas a swelling above suggests lacrimal gland pathology). The positive regurgitation test — pus or mucopurulent material expressed through the punctum on pressing the sac — is characteristic. Pain, fever, and tenderness characterize acute dacryocystitis. Photophobia is not a feature. S-shaped upper eyelid deformity occurs with lacrimal gland tumors.

question: Regarding investigating a case of epiphora which of the following is true?
options: Positive primary Jones dye test indicates complete NLD obstruction
Presence of soft stop indicates patent upper canalicular system
Positive secondary Jones dye test indicates partial NLD obstruction
Positive regurgitation test indicates patent NLD
answer: Positive secondary Jones dye test indicates partial NLD obstruction
tags: Lacrimal System: Questions from End 40 G1 (Batch 2)
explain: The Jones dye tests assess lacrimal drainage:

question: Tear film consists of the following?
options: Outer lipid layer secreted from goblet cells
Inner lipid layer secreted from goblet cells
Intermediate aqueous layer secreted by Krause and Wolfering glands
Inner mucus layer secreted from Moll's glands
answer: Intermediate aqueous layer secreted by Krause and Wolfering glands
tags: Lacrimal System: Questions from End 40 G1 (Batch 2)
explain: The tear film has three layers (from outer to inner):

question: A 45-year-old female with chronic dacryocystitis. Which of the following most likely describes her condition?
options: S shaped deformity of upper eyelid
Swelling below the medial palpebral ligament
answer: Swelling below the medial palpebral ligament
explain: Chronic dacryocystitis is characterized by chronic inflammation of the lacrimal sac secondary to nasolacrimal duct obstruction. The hallmark clinical sign is swelling of the lacrimal sac below the medial palpebral ligament (MPL), along with epiphora, mucopurulent discharge, and a positive regurgitation test (reflux of pus/mucus on pressure over the sac). Severe pain, fever, and tenderness characterize acute dacryocystitis. S-shaped deformity of the upper eyelid is a feature of lacrimal gland tumors (which cause mechanical ptosis with lateral displacement). Blurring of near vision is not a feature of dacryocystitis.

question: A three-month-old infant with a watering eye. On pressing lacrimal sac, regurgitation of muco-pus material occurs. What is the appropriate treatment?
options: Massage with antibiotics up to the age of 6 months
answer: Massage with antibiotics up to the age of 6 months
explain: In a 3-month-old infant, the most common cause of watering eye with muco-purulent regurgitation is congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction — most often due to an imperforate Hasner's valve at the lower end of the nasolacrimal duct. The initial management is conservative: lacrimal sac massage (Crigler massage — pressing downward over the lacrimal sac to build hydraulic pressure that may rupture the membranous obstruction) combined with topical antibiotic drops to control secondary infection. This is continued until 6 months of age, by which time up to 90% of cases resolve spontaneously. Probing is reserved for persistent cases at 6–12 months. DCR is a later surgical option if probing fails.

question: Chronic dacryocystitis is usually associated with?
options: Swelling above the medial palpebral ligament
Swelling below the medial palpebral ligament
answer: Swelling below the medial palpebral ligament
explain: The lacrimal sac lies in the lacrimal fossa in the medial orbital wall, below the medial palpebral ligament. Consequently, swelling from lacrimal sac disease (dacryocystitis or mucocele) appears below the medial palpebral ligament. This anatomical landmark is diagnostically important: swelling below = lacrimal sac; swelling above = different pathology (e.g., angular dermoid, trochlear lesion). Chronic dacryocystitis typically presents with painless epiphora, a palpable swelling below the medial palpebral ligament, and a positive regurgitation test (muco-purulent reflux from the punctum when pressure is applied to the lacrimal sac). Pain, fever, and tenderness characterize acute dacryocystitis.

question: A 45-year-old female with chronic dacryocystitis. Which of the following most likely describes her condition?
options: Swelling below the medial palpebral ligament
answer: Swelling below the medial palpebral ligament
tags: Lacrimal System: Questions from Final 40 Summer (Batch 5)
explain: Chronic dacryocystitis presents with a swelling of the lacrimal sac located below the medial palpebral ligament (MPL). The textbook states the clinical picture includes "swelling of the lacrimal sac below the MPL" and a "regurgitation test +ve: pressure on the lacrimal sac causes regurge of mucus or pus from either the puncta." Pain, fever, and tenderness characterize acute dacryocystitis, not chronic. S-shaped deformity is seen with lacrimal gland tumors. Photophobia is not a feature of dacryocystitis.

question: Tear film consists of the following?
options: Outer lipid layer secreted from goblet cells
Inner lipid layer secreted from goblet cells
Intermediate aqueous layer secreted by Krause and Wolfering glands
Inner mucus layer secreted from Moll's glands
answer: Intermediate aqueous layer secreted by Krause and Wolfering glands
tags: Lacrimal System: Questions from Final 40 Summer (Batch 5)
explain: The precorneal tear film has three layers. The textbook states: "Middle aqueous layer: secreted by lacrimal gland (main & accessory)" — and among the accessory glands are "Krause's gland: located in the upper and lower fornices" and "Wolfring gland: located in the palpebral conjunctiva opposite the mid-tarsus." The outer lipid layer is secreted by Meibomian glands (not goblet cells), and the inner mucin layer is secreted by goblet cells of the conjunctiva (not Moll's glands). Zeiss glands are sebaceous glands related to the lashes, not tear film secretors.

question: The cause of congenital nasolacrimal obstruction is?
options: Maldevelopment of the valve of Rosenmüller
Membranous block of the valve of Hasner
Retention of amniotic fluid in the nasolacrimal sac
answer: Membranous block of the valve of Hasner
tags: Lacrimal System: Questions from Formative Exam (Batch 7)
explain: The textbook states that the nasolacrimal duct ends in the inferior meatus of the nose, guarded by "Hasner's valve — a one-way valve allowing tears down but not air up." Congenital nasolacrimal obstruction is caused by failure of canalization at this valve, resulting in a membranous obstruction. The textbook's treatment protocol for congenital dacryocystitis describes probing to open this obstruction, confirming that the block is at the valve of Hasner.

question: A mother brings her 12-month-old child to you because of chronic right-sided epiphora. She has been massaging the right nasolacrimal sac for the past 6 months; however, the epiphora has persisted. The next therapeutic recommendation would include?
options: Observation, as most congenital obstructions resolve without therapy
tags: Lacrimal System: Questions from Formative Exam (Batch 7)
explain: The textbook describes a stepwise approach to congenital nasolacrimal obstruction: medical treatment with massage for the first 9–12 months, and if no improvement occurs after the age of 1 year, "probing is indicated." This child is 12 months old, has had 6 months of failed massage, and thus meets the criteria for the next step — nasolacrimal system probing. DCR is reserved for cases where probing fails twice. Continued massage or observation alone would not be appropriate at this age given the failed conservative trial.
